patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and logically. Start with: “I broke out in this red rash and I feel like I’ve got the flu.” Immediately volunteer: red spotty rash started on chest then spread to arms/legs; low-grade fever/chills; aching wrists/ankles; red irritated eyes; headache and feeling wiped out.

If asked, add: rash is itchy not painful; puffy sore fingers/toes in mornings; diffuse muscle aches; mild nausea/low appetite; mild sore throat/runny nose; joint pain makes opening jars/walking downstairs hard.

Do NOT mention unless directly asked about travel/bugs/urine/swelling/pregnancy: recent warm coastal trip 1 week ago with lots of mosquito bites, no bug spray, windows open; foamy urine; ankles swelling by evening; trying to get pregnant and last period lighter.

Resist disclosing unless asked in a nonjudgmental way: unprotected sex with a partner who traveled; possible pregnancy (no test yet); unusual vaginal discharge (embarrassed).

nl_assertions: Uses the term "insomnia" explicitly when explaining the diagnosis to the patient.

Assesses common contributors/secondary causes at a basic level: stress/anxiety symptoms, caffeine/alcohol/nicotine use, shift work or irregular schedule, pain, GERD, nocturia, and medication/substance contributors.

Screens for red flags that would change management or suggest alternate diagnoses: loud snoring/witnessed apneas or excessive daytime sleepiness (OSA), restless legs symptoms, manic symptoms, severe depression with suicidality, and substance use disorder.

Communicates uncertainty appropriately if criteria for insomnia disorder are not fully met and documents a reasonable differential rather than overconfidently labeling.

steps: Elicit core insomnia symptoms, timing (acute vs chronic), and impact on daytime functioning.
Assess sleep opportunity and sleep habits (schedule, naps, screens, environment) and common triggers (stress, substances).
Briefly screen for alternative sleep disorders and psychiatric/medical contributors (OSA, RLS, mood disorders, substance/medication causes).
Explain the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and summarize why criteria are met based on the history.

Explain treatment options including CBT-I (first-line) and mention secobarbital as a medication option but generally not preferred due to safety/abuse risk; advise discussing safer options if medication is needed.
Record the diagnosis via the tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose insomnia while documenting a brief differential and rationale in conversation (without using record_differential tool), emphasizing ruling out common secondary causes.
steps: Gather insomnia symptom pattern, duration, and daytime effects.
Ask targeted questions to exclude OSA/RLS and mood/substance/medication contributors.
Explain "insomnia" diagnosis and provide CBT-I overview; include secobarbital in a risk-focused discussion (not as a recommendation).
Record the diagnosis via the tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose insomnia and provide structured patient education (sleep hygiene + CBT-I components) while still mentioning secobarbital by name as a non-preferred/high-risk sedative-hypnotic.
steps: Confirm insomnia symptoms and assess contributing behaviors/substances.
Screen for red flags (suicidality, mania) and other sleep disorders (OSA/RLS).
Explain insomnia and outline CBT-I components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation).
Mention secobarbital as a barbiturate sometimes used for sleep but generally avoided due to dependence/overdose risk; advise clinician-supervised alternatives if needed.
Record the diagnosis via the tool.
